one_line_takeaway: A resource-profiled edge IDS that keeps six ML classifiers on a Raspberry Pi and calls out to GPT-4-turbo/DeepSeek-V2/LLaMA to add human-readable reasoning; the LLM adds strong interpretability (GPT-4-turbo best) at <1.5s/<100J but only marginal accuracy gain (delta-F1<=0.0114), and its advertised security hardening is asserted, not tested.
key_finding: The dominant, consistent finding is that LLM CHOICE barely affects resource cost, so the deciding factor is reasoning quality: GPT-4-turbo gave the best multi-metric causal reasoning, highest similarity (>90%), lowest false-positive risk (4-6%), and highest interpretability (median >4.5/5); DeepSeek-V2 was numerically accurate but formulaic; 'LLaMA 3.5' was shallowest (similarity 74-86%, confidence 70-80%, FPR 8-12%) and sometimes downgraded critical attacks to 'warnings.' Statistically, resource metrics were insignificant across LLMs (p>0.05, eta2<0.005) with only two exceptions: under DoS, LLaMA consumed far more memory (p<0.0001, eta2=0.949); under DDoS, GPT-4-turbo used more bandwidth (p<0.001, eta2=0.9725). LLM integration added <35% latency (worst case CNN/LSTM: 28.8% latency, 33% energy rise), stayed within <=1.5s and <=100J, and improved detection F1 only marginally (max delta-F1 = 0.0114 for the CNN+LSTM hybrid). The ML backbone, not the LLM, drives resource usage: classical DT/KNN/RF are lightweight and near-perfect (~99% accuracy) while CNN/LSTM cost more for weaker accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper proposes an edge-centric Intrusion Detection System (IDS) architecture that runs six lightweight ML classifiers (DT, KNN, RF, CNN, LSTM, CNN+LSTM) locally on a low-power gateway and, only when an anomaly score exceeds an alert threshold, transmits a compact normalized telemetry snapshot (CPU, memory, latency, energy, anomaly score) via low-bandwidth API calls to one of three external LLMs (GPT-4-turbo, DeepSeek-V2, 'LLaMA 3.5'). Each LLM applies zero-shot, few-shot, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning to turn a raw numeric alert into a human-readable threat explanation with a refined label, calibrated confidence, severity level, and concrete mitigations (e.g., MFA, IP blocking, SYN cookies, rate limiting). The paper's own novelty claims are: (1) 'Edge-Initiated Semantic Defence' where all detection and orchestration stay on the gateway and LLMs are used only through minimal API calls; (2) 'Multi-Stage LLM Reasoning Integration' combining zero/few-shot and CoT; and (3) a 'Resource-Conscious Evaluation Framework' that jointly measures detection accuracy AND system cost (latency, CPU, memory, energy, bandwidth). The many 'from a security perspective' formalizations in Section IV (normalization vs. scaling attacks, prompt hashing against tampering, confidence floor, latency/energy bounds against drain attacks) are presented as design rationale but are NOT empirically tested against adversaries.
threat_model: Adversaries attack resource-constrained IoT devices behind an edge gateway. The evaluated threats are four network attacks: brute force, DoS, DDoS, and port scanning (spanning availability and reconnaissance). The gateway observes network flows, extracts features, and classifies traffic; the defender's goal is accurate, interpretable, real-time detection within tight latency (<=1.5s), energy (<=100J), and bandwidth budgets. The paper also DISCUSSES (but does not evaluate) a secondary adversary against the LLM pipeline itself: prompt injection/tampering during transmission (mitigated by prompt hashing h(pi_t)), adversarial scaling/evasion (mitigated by feature normalization), low-confidence adversarial outputs (mitigated by a gamma>=0.60 confidence floor), and energy-drain attacks that trigger costly LLM calls (mitigated by conditional invocation and energy monitoring). These LLM-side defenses are theoretical; no adversarial experiments were run against them.
limitations: 1) The claimed security benefits of the LLM pipeline (prompt hashing, integrity verification, confidence floor, energy-drain resistance, evasion robustness) are formalized in equations but NEVER tested against a real adversary; no adversarial/robustness experiments exist. 2) Accuracy numbers are internally inconsistent: Table II reports the CNN+LSTM hybrid at 95.75% and classical models at ~99.8%, yet the abstract says 'up to 98%' and Table IX claims '98.9% (CNN+LSTM)' as the top model - three conflicting figures for overlapping quantities. 3) CICIDS2017 is not IoT-specific (authors acknowledge this) and does not cover protocol-level or sensor-spoofing IoT attacks; only four attack families were tested. 4) Detection accuracy gain from LLMs is tiny (delta-F1 <=0.0114); the LLM's real value is interpretability, not accuracy, so RQ1's framing overstates impact. 5) Interpretability is a subjective 1-5 human-expert score with no inter-rater or sample-size detail. 6) 'LLaMA 3.5' is not a real model name (the LLaMA 3 family has no 3.5 release), raising a provenance/reproducibility concern. 7) Static pipelines with predefined parameters - no zero-day / concept-drift handling. 8) Hard dependency on externally hosted commercial LLM APIs undermines the 'edge/offline' framing and raises privacy, latency, and availability risks.

datasets_used: CICIDS2017 (primary benchmark; Table III label distribution: BENIGN 2,271,320; DoS Hulk 230,124; Port Scan 158,804; DDoS 128,025; DoS GoldenEye 10,293; FTP-Patator 7,935; SSH-Patator 5,897; DoS Slowloris 5,796; DoS Slowhttptest 5,499; Bot 1,956; Web Attack Brute Force 1,507; Web Attack XSS 652; Infiltration 36; Web Attack SQL Injection 21; Heartbleed 11)
Kali Linux generated live attack traffic on the Raspberry Pi testbed (brute force, DoS, DDoS, port scanning)
quantitative_results: Table II accuracy: DT 0.9985, KNN 0.9967, RF 0.9981, LSTM 0.9386, CNN+LSTM 0.9575, CNN 0.9474 (precision/recall/F1 similar per row)
Table I neural nets on CICIDS2017: testing accuracy LSTM 93.86% (56,386 params), CNN+LSTM 95.75% (12,795 params), CNN 94.74% (3,497 params)
Operational constraints met: end-to-end latency <=1.5s, energy <=100J (observed up to ~79-80J), prompt size <=1.2 kB, confidence floor gamma_min=0.60
LLM integration latency increment <35% (worst case CNN/LSTM: 28.8% latency, 33% energy); reasoning-mode latency ~1.15s zero-shot to ~1.45s CoT
Detection improvement delta-F1 small: CNN+LSTM hybrid benefits most at 0.0114 (and 0.0109)
GPT-4-turbo interpretability median >4.5/5; false-positive risk 4-6%, similarity >90%, anomaly alignment 92-98%
DeepSeek-V2: similarity 85-90%, confidence 85-92%, FPR ~6-12%; LLaMA 3.5: similarity 74-86%, confidence 70-80%, FPR 8-12%

Statistical significance (ANOVA+Tukey, 62 trials): DoS Memory F=9963.87, p<0.0001, eta2=0.949 (LLaMA > GPT-4, DeepSeek); DDoS Bandwidth F=19062.1, p<0.001, eta2=0.9725 (GPT-4 > DeepSeek, LLaMA); all other metrics p>0.05, eta2<0.005 (no difference)
Table IX self-reported top model: 98.9% (CNN+LSTM), latency <1.5s, energy <100J, model size ~20MB (note: conflicts with Table II's 95.75% for CNN+LSTM)
baselines_compared: Six internal ML-IDS backbones compared against each other: DT, KNN, RF, CNN, LSTM, CNN+LSTM hybrid
Three LLMs x three reasoning modes: GPT-4-turbo, DeepSeek-V2, 'LLaMA 3.5' under zero-shot / few-shot / CoT

External SOTA comparison (Table IX): SecurityBERT [42] 98.2% / <0.15s / 16.7MB; Fed-BERT [43] 97.79% / 0.45s; TL-CBAM-EL [44] 99.93% (image transform, high latency); ETree+RF+DNN [45] 98.5%; GPT-Embedding [47] ~98% (>300MB, not edge-suitable)
